title: Top 5 Movie Duos and Why ‘The Last Duel’ Rules
format: Sean and Amanda make the case for a $100 million Ridley Scott epic nobody went to see, draft dueling top-five movie-duo lists, and Todd Haynes stops by to talk about turning the Velvet Underground into a documentary.

segments.heading: The Last Duel
segments.note: Ridley Scott, $5 million opening weekend, a lot to defend

films.title: The Last Duel
films.note: Matt Damon and Ben Affleck's first produced screenplay together since Good Will Hunting (with Nicole Holofcener), based on Eric Jager's 2004 nonfiction book. Damon plays medieval knight Jean de Carrouges, Jodie Comer his wife Marguerite, and Adam Driver the nobleman she accuses of rape, a charge settled under 14th-century French law by trial by combat. Told in three chapters, one per character's account, each headed 'the truth according to...' until Marguerite's, which drops the qualifier. Both hosts loved it; it opened to $5 million against a $100 million budget, which they spend most of the segment trying to make sense of.

films.title: Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid
films.note: No. 1. Robert Redford and Paul Newman, who made only two movies together (this and The Sting) but treat each other as equals throughout. Sean says the chemistry comes from neither one needing to win every scene.

films.title: Toy Story
films.note: No. 2. Woody and Buzz, voiced by Tom Hanks and Tim Allen, though Sean picks the characters rather than the actors. His nephew's obsession with the movies got him reconnected to them.

films.title: The Thin Man
films.note: No. 3. Myrna Loy and William Powell, picked on behalf of Sean's wife Eileen, a devotee of the boozy detective franchise (based on the Dashiell Hammett novel, directed by W.S. Van Dyke) that ran for years afterward.

films.title: Talladega Nights: The Ballad of Ricky Bobby
films.note: Tied for No. 4 with Richard Pryor and Gene Wilder. Will Ferrell and John C. Reilly, who Sean says need to make a third movie together after this and Step Brothers.

films.title: See No Evil, Hear No Evil
films.note: Tied for No. 4 with Will Ferrell and John C. Reilly. Not necessarily the best Richard Pryor/Gene Wilder movie, but the one Sean watched on HBO as a kid that made the pairing click for him.

films.title: Cheech & Chong's Next Movie
films.note: No. 5. Cheech Marin and Tommy Chong. Sean isn't a stoner or a weed guy, he says, just someone who loves the absurd premises and the punchlines.

films.title: Adam's Rib
films.note: No. 1. Katharine Hepburn and Spencer Tracy: the run of Woman of the Year, Adam's Rib, Pat and Mike, Desk Set, and Guess Who's Coming to Dinner is, Amanda says, the best of any screen duo. Adam's Rib is her pick for the best of the bunch, Pat and Mike her sentimental favorite.

films.title: Top Hat
films.note: No. 2. Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers, who made ten films together; Amanda says she remembers the dance sequences more than any individual movie, alongside Swing Time and Shall We Dance.

films.title: Sleepless in Seattle
films.note: No. 3. Tom Hanks and Meg Ryan, quintessential modern romantic comedy chemistry, also covering Joe Versus the Volcano and You've Got Mail.

films.title: Good Will Hunting
films.note: No. 4. Matt Damon and Ben Affleck. Amanda traces the impact of the Good Will Hunting press tour on her own moviegoing, plus School Ties, Chasing Amy, Dogma, and the EuroTrip cameo.

films.title: Bad Boys for Life
films.note: No. 5. Will Smith and Martin Lawrence, her franchise pick. She almost went with Will Smith and Tommy Lee Jones instead, but Men in Black 3 didn't do it for her.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.note: The Velvet Underground, streaming on Apple TV+

films.title: The Velvet Underground
films.note: Haynes' first documentary, produced with Christine Vachon and Motto Films for Apple TV+. Built around interviews with people who were actually there (John Cale first, then Jonas Mekas just before his death) rather than decades of admirers, and styled after the experimental, multi-frame film language of Warhol and his circle rather than a conventional talking-head structure. Lou Reed, who died in 2013, appears mostly through archival tape, including phone calls he made to Danny Fields.

films.title: Safe
films.note: Haynes' 1995 film had a resurgence during COVID lockdowns; he says he didn't rewatch it himself but heard from people finding its themes of contagion and isolation newly resonant.

referenced.group: Comparisons in The Last Duel review
